subject: Letter from the Eastern Front
excerpt: The distances here are incomprehensible. We have marched further in the last weeks than I could have believed possible...
full_text:
Dearest Family,

I write to you from somewhere near Vyazma, where we have halted to consolidate. The distances here are incomprehensible — we have marched and ridden further in the last weeks than I could have believed possible. Russia is not like France. The land goes on without end, and the autumn mud has already begun. My boots have not been dry in a week.

Despite all this, spirits remain high. The men around me are good soldiers and good comrades. We have seen hard fighting, but we believe in what we are doing. The encirclement battles have been enormous — tens of thousands of prisoners streaming westward, more than I can count.

I hope Father's health is improving. Tell Mother her package arrived with the socks and the Lebkuchen — they were shared with the whole section and gone in minutes. Everyone sends their thanks.

I cannot say when I will next write, but know that I think of home every day.

Your Hans
notes: Gefreiter Becker was killed in action near Rzhev in January 1942. This letter was returned to his family after the war.

subject: Final letter from the Stalingrad Pocket
excerpt: I no longer believe we will be relieved. The Manstein attempt has failed. I ask only that you remember me as I was in better days...
full_text:
My dear Helga,

I write this knowing it may be my last letter. The situation is clear to every man here who is honest with himself — I no longer believe we will be relieved. The relief attempt has failed. The rations now are almost nothing; we burn furniture for warmth.

I do not write this to cause you distress, but because I believe you deserve the truth from me. I have conducted myself with honour. I have not surrendered though I might have. I believe that counts for something.

If God wills that I do not return, please raise the children to know that their father loved them above all things. Do not teach them hatred. The world we were told we were fighting for has not come to pass.

Kiss the children for me. I see your face each night when I sleep.

Your Karl
notes: Müller surrendered on 2 February 1943 and died in Soviet captivity in March 1943. This letter was part of the last mail airlift from the Stalingrad pocket.

subject: From Paris — after the victory
excerpt: Father, we drove into Paris this morning. The boulevards are empty and silent. I confess I felt something complicated standing beneath the Arc de Triomphe...
full_text:
Dear Father,

I write from Paris — it is still strange to write those words. We drove in this morning with the column; the boulevards are empty and largely silent. A few French civilians watch from shuttered windows. I confess I felt something complicated standing beneath the Arc de Triomphe in uniform. Greatness visited by greatness, perhaps, though I know that is not how they will see it.

The campaign was everything the training had promised and more. The Panzer felt like thunder rolling across the earth. The French were not cowards — they fought in places — but they could not adjust. We were always somewhere they did not expect.

General Rommel was remarkable. I saw his command vehicle once during the crossing of the Meuse. He was right at the front, watching the engineers. No hesitation.

I hope we have seen the last of the fighting. Perhaps now a negotiated peace will be possible, and I can come home.

Your Fritz
notes: Von Schiller survived the war and emigrated to Argentina in 1947.

subject: After the invasion
excerpt: The weight of the Allied material is something no one here was prepared for. We hold our positions, but each attack costs us men we cannot replace...
full_text:
Dear Lotte,

I have little time to write, but I want you to know I am alive. The situation in Normandy is not what the radio describes. The weight of Allied material — tanks, artillery, aircraft above all — is something no one here was fully prepared for. We hold our positions, but each attack costs us men we cannot replace. The replacements when they come are boys.

I will not write what I truly think of our chances. You will understand what my silence means.

I ask only one thing: if anything happens to me, see that my books go to the university library in Freiburg. Father will know which ones.

Think of me on Sundays.

Your Werner
notes: Hauptmann Haas was wounded at Falaise in August 1944 and survived the war. He became a schoolteacher in Baden-Württemberg.
